content:
First observe that for fixed \(a<391\) one has

\[
\text{lcm}(a,391)\;=\;a\cdot\frac{391}{\gcd(a,391)}
\quad\text{and}\quad
\text{lcm}(a,b)\;=\;a\cdot\frac{b}{\gcd(a,b)}\,.
\]

We want
\[
\text{lcm}(a,b)\;>\;\text{lcm}(a,391)
\;\Longleftrightarrow\;
a\;\frac{b}{\gcd(a,b)}\;>\;a\;\frac{391}{\gcd(a,391)}
\;\Longleftrightarrow\;
\frac{b}{\gcd(a,b)}\;>\;\frac{391}{\gcd(a,391)}.
\]

In particular, one sees that the only hope for small \(b\) is to introduce a prime factor
in \(b\) which “beats” the corresponding factor in \(391=17\cdot23\).  Checking primes in order,

– \(b=2,3,5,7,11,13\) all fail the inequality for every \(a<391\).  
– The prime \(19\) works:  take for instance \(a=23\).  Then
  \(\gcd(23,19)=1\) and \(\gcd(23,391)=23\), so
  \[
     \text{lcm}(23,19)\;=\;23\cdot19\;=\;437
     \;>\; \text{lcm}(23,391)\;=\;391.
  \]
Thus \((a,b)=(23,19)\) is a “pupusa” and no smaller \(b\) can succeed.

Hence the minimum possible \(b\) is \(\boxed{19}\).
